entries.title: Canonical remediation for SQL injection
entries.canonical_fix_summary: Keep untrusted values out of SQL text with parameter binding or ORM-safe APIs, constrain dynamic query parts to allow-lists, and verify that attacker-controlled input can no longer change query semantics.

framework_variants.summary: Use parameterized queries in `pg`, `mysql2`, Prisma, or Sequelize and never concatenate request data into SQL strings.
recommended_actions: Replace string-built SQL with placeholders and a separate values array.
Allow-list any dynamic sort column or table selector before it reaches the query builder.

framework_variants.summary: Prefer SQLAlchemy ORM or parameter binding through SQLAlchemy text constructs instead of interpolating request data into raw SQL.
recommended_actions: Move request values into bound parameters instead of f-strings or percent formatting.
Keep raw SQL behind reviewed helper functions that reject dynamic structural tokens.

framework_variants.summary: Use the Django ORM by default and parameterize any unavoidable raw queries through framework-supported bindings.
recommended_actions: Replace raw query string assembly with ORM filters where possible.
If raw SQL remains, pass parameters separately and never build WHERE clauses from user strings.

framework_variants.summary: Use prepared statements, named parameters, or JPA parameter binding so request values never become SQL syntax.
recommended_actions: Switch JDBC string concatenation to prepared statements or named parameter helpers.
Constrain dynamic sort fields or repository selectors with server-side enums or allow-lists.

framework_variants.summary: Use parameterized `SqlCommand`, Dapper parameters, or EF Core LINQ instead of concatenated SQL.
recommended_actions: Move external input into typed command parameters rather than interpolated SQL.
Prefer EF Core LINQ or stored procedures for complex filtering when practical.

framework_variants.summary: Treat SQL injection as a server-side fix; client components should not be presented as the security boundary.
recommended_actions: Apply the remediation in route handlers, server actions, or API services that build queries.
Do not rely on client-side validation or escaping as a substitute for parameterized execution.

validation_steps: Run the vulnerable request with SQL metacharacters and confirm the query still behaves as a normal data lookup.
Add a regression test that passes attacker-controlled input into the same code path and asserts parameter binding is used.
Review logging or query traces to confirm untrusted input is emitted as data values, not merged into SQL text.
unsafe_alternatives: Escaping quotes manually instead of using parameterized execution.
Relying on client-side validation or hidden form fields to keep SQL safe.
Switching to a different query string template without changing the dataflow model.

references.label: OWASP SQL Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet
CWE-89 Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an SQL Command

sources.label: OWASP SQL Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet
CWE-89 Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an SQL Command

entries.title: Canonical remediation for command injection
entries.canonical_fix_summary: Remove shell-string execution where possible, call safe library APIs directly, and when process execution is unavoidable pass fixed executables with validated argument lists that keep untrusted data out of command syntax.

framework_variants.summary: Prefer Node libraries over `exec()` or shell-based helpers and pass fixed binaries with validated argument arrays when process execution is unavoidable.
recommended_actions: Replace `exec()` with a safer API path or fixed-command execution using validated arguments.
Allow-list every user-influenced flag, filename, or mode before it becomes a process argument.

framework_variants.summary: Avoid `subprocess` shell execution on request data and move user choices into explicit, validated application-level operations.
recommended_actions: Remove `shell=True` and stop building command strings from path or query parameters.
If invoking an external binary is required, validate each argument against a strict allow-list.

framework_variants.summary: Handle user actions through Python application logic instead of shell commands, especially in admin or upload workflows.
recommended_actions: Replace shell-based file or utility operations with native Python libraries where possible.
If subprocess execution remains, isolate the executable and restrict arguments to known-safe values.

framework_variants.summary: Use Java libraries for file, archive, or system operations and avoid shell composition through runtime process APIs with untrusted input.
recommended_actions: Move user-controlled behavior into enum-backed operations instead of command text.
Normalize and validate every argument before it reaches process execution.

framework_variants.summary: Avoid piping request data into shell execution and use managed APIs instead of `cmd.exe`, PowerShell, or interpolated process strings.
recommended_actions: Replace shell command composition with direct framework APIs or fixed executable invocations.
Validate any remaining arguments and disable shell interpretation paths.

framework_variants.summary: Treat command injection as a server-side concern in route handlers, server actions, background jobs, or API services.
recommended_actions: Move the fix to the server code that launches the process, not the client component that triggered it.
Do not rely on disabled buttons or client-side field constraints as command injection controls.

validation_steps: Replay the attacker-controlled input and confirm it can no longer change the executed command, flags, or separator behavior.
Add regression coverage for malicious arguments such as shell metacharacters, path traversal tokens, or extra flags.
Confirm the final code path uses native libraries or fixed executable-plus-argument execution rather than shell string parsing.
unsafe_alternatives: Escaping a few shell characters but continuing to build command strings from user input.
Relying on front-end restrictions to prevent dangerous values from reaching the server.
Switching from one shell helper to another without removing user control over the command text.

references.label: OWASP OS Command Injection Defense Cheat Sheet
CWE-78 Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command

sources.label: OWASP OS Command Injection Defense Cheat Sheet
CWE-78 Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command

entries.title: Canonical remediation for weak JWT validation
entries.canonical_fix_summary: Verify signature, issuer, audience, expiry, and accepted algorithms on every token, and reject tokens that are unsigned, weakly validated, or trusted only because they exist.

framework_variants.summary: Configure JWT verification middleware to require allowed algorithms, issuer, audience, and lifetime checks rather than relying on library defaults.
recommended_actions: Pass explicit verification options for issuer, audience, and accepted algorithms in the middleware or auth helper.
Reject unsigned or weakly validated tokens before controller logic runs.

framework_variants.summary: Validate JWT claims explicitly in the dependency or auth layer and bind accepted algorithms and issuer or audience checks to configuration you control.
recommended_actions: Enforce issuer, audience, expiry, and algorithm validation when decoding tokens.
Separate token parsing from trust decisions so presence of a token is never enough.

framework_variants.summary: Require full token validation in authentication backends or DRF auth classes, including claim validation and accepted signing algorithms.
recommended_actions: Centralize JWT verification in one reviewed backend instead of scattered helper code.
Reject tokens with missing or unexpected issuer, audience, expiry, or algorithm claims.

framework_variants.summary: Use Spring Security resource-server or equivalent configuration to pin issuer, audience policy, and accepted signing algorithms.
recommended_actions: Configure JWT decoders with explicit issuer and key expectations.
Apply audience and expiry validation before the token is converted into authorities.

framework_variants.summary: Use `TokenValidationParameters` to require issuer, audience, signing key, lifetime, and algorithm policy checks.
recommended_actions: Set issuer, audience, and lifetime validation to required instead of permissive defaults.
Ensure tokens are rejected when signature or algorithm policy does not match the trusted configuration.

framework_variants.summary: Treat JWT validation as a server-side responsibility in route handlers, middleware, or the upstream API rather than a client-side trust decision.
recommended_actions: Apply strict verification where the session is established or protected server data is fetched.
Do not trust decoded client-side claims as authorization truth.

validation_steps: Test expired, wrong-issuer, wrong-audience, and unexpected-algorithm tokens and confirm each one is rejected.
Add automated tests that prove protected routes fail closed when claim validation is incomplete.
Review authentication middleware configuration to confirm signature and claim validation occur before authorization logic.
unsafe_alternatives: Decoding JWTs without verification and treating decoded claims as trusted identity.
Checking expiry alone while skipping issuer, audience, or algorithm validation.
Using the token's declared algorithm as the only source of truth for what to accept.

references.label: OWASP JSON Web Token Cheat Sheet for Java
CWE-345 Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity

sources.label: OWASP JSON Web Token Cheat Sheet for Java
CWE-345 Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity

entries.title: Canonical remediation for reflected cross-site scripting
entries.canonical_fix_summary: Apply context-appropriate output encoding by default, sanitize rich HTML only when genuinely required, and ensure untrusted data cannot reach HTML, script, URL, or attribute sinks without the right protection for that sink.

framework_variants.summary: Prefer templating and response helpers that escape by default, and sanitize any HTML rendering path that intentionally accepts rich content.
recommended_actions: Replace direct HTML concatenation with auto-escaping templates or safe response encoding.
If rich HTML is required, sanitize before rendering and keep dangerous sinks out of controller code.

framework_variants.summary: Return data through templating or JSON layers that keep untrusted input out of raw HTML sinks unless explicitly sanitized.
recommended_actions: Avoid injecting request values into raw HTML responses without context-aware encoding.
Use sanitization and strict templates for any feature that renders user-provided markup.

framework_variants.summary: Rely on Django auto-escaping by default and avoid raw-output bypasses for untrusted content.
recommended_actions: Remove unsafe template bypasses on data derived from request input.
Sanitize stored or reflected rich text before intentionally rendering it as HTML.

framework_variants.summary: Use templating engines and view helpers that escape output by default and keep user input out of direct script or HTML sinks.
recommended_actions: Remove direct HTML assembly in controllers or views when request data is reflected.
Sanitize any unavoidable rich HTML path and keep it isolated from generic rendering.

framework_variants.summary: Use Razor encoding defaults and avoid raw HTML helpers on untrusted data unless sanitized through a reviewed path.
recommended_actions: Replace raw output helpers with encoded rendering for request-derived values.
Constrain and sanitize any feature that intentionally supports rich user HTML.

framework_variants.summary: Keep untrusted data in JSX text positions by default and avoid `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` unless the content is sanitized through a reviewed path.
recommended_actions: Render attacker-controlled values as text rather than HTML.
If `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` is unavoidable, sanitize the content and isolate the sink behind a reviewed component.

validation_steps: Replay the payload with script tags or event-handler input and confirm the browser renders inert text or sanitized markup instead of executing code.
Add regression coverage for the exact sink that previously reflected attacker-controlled data.
Review all equivalent render paths to ensure the same encoding or sanitization policy is consistently applied.
unsafe_alternatives: Encoding some characters manually while leaving context-specific sinks unprotected.
Trusting framework auto-escaping after explicitly opting out with raw HTML helpers.
Relying on client-side validation alone to keep dangerous markup out of the response.

references.label: OWASP Cross Site Scripting Prevention Cheat Sheet
CWE-79 Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation

sources.label: OWASP Cross Site Scripting Prevention Cheat Sheet
CWE-79 Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation

entries.title: Canonical remediation for path traversal
entries.canonical_fix_summary: Bind file access to a fixed base directory or server-side identifier map, normalize and resolve paths before use, and reject any request-derived path that escapes the allowed boundary or selects unexpected files.

framework_variants.summary: Resolve request-derived filenames against a fixed base path and reject traversal attempts before file-system access occurs.
recommended_actions: Map user choices to known-safe filenames or identifiers instead of accepting raw paths.
Normalize and verify the resolved path remains under the intended storage root before reading or writing.

framework_variants.summary: Treat path parameters and query inputs as untrusted selectors and bind them to controlled storage locations or identifiers.
recommended_actions: Resolve user input against a fixed base directory and reject any normalized path that escapes it.
Use server-side identifiers for downloads or uploads instead of raw filesystem paths.

framework_variants.summary: Keep request data out of direct filesystem paths and serve files through reviewed storage abstractions or allow-listed names.
recommended_actions: Translate request values into known-safe object keys rather than concatenated paths.
Normalize and boundary-check any remaining file path before storage access.

framework_variants.summary: Resolve file requests against a fixed application-controlled root and verify the canonical path never leaves that root.
recommended_actions: Replace raw path concatenation with canonical path checks and allow-listed resources.
Use storage services that expose identifiers instead of arbitrary filesystem paths.

framework_variants.summary: Normalize request-derived paths, compare them to a fixed trusted base directory, and reject any path that escapes the expected root.
recommended_actions: Use server-side identifiers or storage abstractions instead of path strings from the request.
Enforce canonical path prefix checks before file read, write, or archive operations.

framework_variants.summary: Treat path traversal remediation as a server-side fix in route handlers, uploads, downloads, or API utilities rather than a client-side validation problem.
recommended_actions: Apply path normalization and boundary checks in the server code that touches storage.
Do not rely on hidden inputs or restricted file pickers as the path traversal control.

validation_steps: Test traversal payloads such as `../`, encoded traversal, and platform-specific separators and confirm the request is rejected.
Add regression tests that assert resolved paths always remain under the intended storage root.
Review adjacent archive, upload, download, and temp-file code paths for the same normalization and boundary-check rules.
unsafe_alternatives: Removing only a few traversal tokens while still accepting arbitrary paths.
Checking string prefixes before normalization or canonical resolution.
Assuming UI constraints or route structure prevent malicious path input from reaching the filesystem.

references.label: OWASP Path Traversal
CWE-22 Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory

sources.label: OWASP Path Traversal
CWE-22 Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory

entries.title: Canonical remediation for SSRF
entries.canonical_fix_summary: Treat outbound destinations as control data: route requests through approved hosts or named integrations, validate protocol and hostname before use, and block internal address ranges, metadata endpoints, redirects, and attacker-controlled protocols.

framework_variants.summary: Treat request-supplied URLs as untrusted control data and route outbound access through allow-listed hosts or named integrations.
recommended_actions: Resolve user choices to approved upstream services instead of fetching arbitrary URLs.
Validate protocol, hostname, and resolved IP before issuing the request and block internal ranges.

framework_variants.summary: Bind external fetch behavior to trusted service definitions rather than direct request parameters or body values.
recommended_actions: Parse and validate URLs before any outbound call and reject unexpected schemes or internal destinations.
Centralize outbound HTTP helpers so metadata and internal-network blocking is enforced consistently.

framework_variants.summary: Move user-controlled fetch targets behind reviewed allow-lists and isolate outbound access behind a helper with hostname and IP validation.
recommended_actions: Map user input to trusted integration identifiers instead of arbitrary URLs.
Block redirects or revalidation paths that can pivot an allowed hostname into an internal address.

framework_variants.summary: Constrain outbound client calls through explicit service configuration and validate final destinations after DNS resolution and redirects.
recommended_actions: Replace arbitrary URL fetch endpoints with named, approved upstream definitions.
Enforce protocol, host, and IP-range allow-lists in the HTTP client layer.

framework_variants.summary: Keep request-derived URLs out of direct `HttpClient` execution and validate protocol, host, and resolved address in one reviewed outbound gateway.
recommended_actions: Use server-side identifiers for integrations instead of raw URLs from the request.
Reject link-local, loopback, RFC1918, and metadata endpoints before outbound requests.

framework_variants.summary: Treat SSRF remediation as a server-side responsibility in route handlers, server actions, or API services, not a client-side validation problem.
recommended_actions: Apply allow-list and network-boundary checks in the server code that issues outbound requests.
Do not trust client-side URL validation as an SSRF control.

validation_steps: Test internal-address and metadata-endpoint payloads and confirm the server rejects them before any outbound request occurs.
Add regression tests for redirects, alternate IP encodings, and unexpected protocols.
Review outbound request helpers to confirm hostname and resolved-IP validation occur in one consistent path.
unsafe_alternatives: Checking only for `http` or `https` while allowing arbitrary hosts.
Allow-listing hostnames without validating final resolved IPs or redirects.
Relying on client-side URL restrictions to keep server-side fetches safe.

references.label: OWASP Server Side Request Forgery Prevention Cheat Sheet
CWE-918 Server-Side Request Forgery

sources.label: OWASP Server Side Request Forgery Prevention Cheat Sheet
CWE-918 Server-Side Request Forgery

entries.title: Canonical remediation for insecure deserialization
entries.canonical_fix_summary: Avoid unsafe object deserializers for untrusted input, prefer data-only formats, and ensure serialized content is validated, signed, or constrained before any object materialization occurs.

framework_variants.summary: Keep untrusted payloads in safe JSON-like structures and avoid flows that deserialize attacker-controlled objects into executable or privileged types.
recommended_actions: Replace unsafe deserializers with JSON parsing plus schema validation.
Reject or sign serialized payloads before any trust boundary crossing.

framework_variants.summary: Use Pydantic or equivalent validation on incoming payloads and keep untrusted data in plain validated structures rather than deserialized object graphs.
recommended_actions: Remove unsafe pickle or binary object loading from request paths.
Constrain accepted payload types to reviewed schemas before processing.

framework_variants.summary: Avoid deserializing untrusted serialized objects in views, background tasks, or session-like flows unless the payload is signed and schema-constrained.
recommended_actions: Replace unsafe object loading with validated JSON or framework-safe serializers.
Reject user-controlled serialized blobs that can instantiate arbitrary object structures.

framework_variants.summary: Disable unsafe polymorphic deserialization paths and restrict accepted classes or message converters to reviewed safe types.
recommended_actions: Configure serializers to reject untrusted type metadata.
Bind request payloads to explicit DTOs instead of arbitrary object graphs.

framework_variants.summary: Use safe serializers with explicit type expectations and avoid binary or unrestricted object deserialization for untrusted input.
recommended_actions: Remove unsafe binary formatter-style flows from user-controlled paths.
Constrain deserialization to known DTOs with validation before business logic.

framework_variants.summary: Treat insecure deserialization as a server-side concern in API handlers, server actions, and session-processing code.
recommended_actions: Keep client payloads in validated data-only formats.
Do not deserialize attacker-controlled server payloads into privileged objects without schema validation.

validation_steps: Replay malicious serialized payloads and confirm they are rejected before object creation or code execution paths.
Add tests proving only expected DTO or schema-bound types are accepted.
Review session, cache, queue, and file-import flows for the same unsafe deserialization pattern.
unsafe_alternatives: Allowing unsafe object loaders because the payload is base64-encoded or compressed.
Checking a few fields after deserialization instead of constraining the deserializer itself.
Assuming internal APIs are safe enough to accept unrestricted serialized objects.

references.label: OWASP Deserialization Cheat Sheet
CWE-502 Deserialization of Untrusted Data

sources.label: OWASP Deserialization Cheat Sheet
CWE-502 Deserialization of Untrusted Data

entries.title: Canonical remediation for weak authentication policy
entries.canonical_fix_summary: Require strong credential policy, brute-force resistance, and multifactor or hardened identity controls where appropriate, and centralize authentication enforcement so weak defaults are not scattered across routes or services.

framework_variants.summary: Enforce password policy, login throttling, lockout, and optional MFA in one reviewed authentication layer instead of route-by-route drift.
recommended_actions: Apply rate limiting and password policy checks at account creation and login.
Prefer hardened identity providers or shared auth middleware over bespoke route-level checks.

framework_variants.summary: Centralize credential and login policy in dependencies or auth services, including throttling, MFA hooks, and password strength enforcement.
recommended_actions: Reject weak passwords during registration and reset flows.
Add login throttling or challenge escalation on repeated failures.

framework_variants.summary: Use Django authentication controls or a hardened identity provider to enforce password rules, throttling, and MFA consistently.
recommended_actions: Enable strong password validators and rate-limiting around login endpoints.
Keep sensitive auth decisions inside one reviewed backend or provider integration.

framework_variants.summary: Use Spring Security policies for password controls, lockout, MFA integration, and request throttling around authentication endpoints.
recommended_actions: Centralize login, password, and MFA policy in the security configuration.
Add brute-force resistance and review any weak fallback or development-only auth path.

framework_variants.summary: Use ASP.NET Identity or an equivalent reviewed auth system to enforce password, lockout, MFA, and challenge policy consistently.
recommended_actions: Enable lockout and strong password requirements in the identity configuration.
Avoid duplicating auth policy in controllers where drift can create weaker paths.

framework_variants.summary: Treat weak authentication remediation as a server and identity-plane change; client UI can support it but should not define the security policy.
recommended_actions: Enforce password and MFA rules in the backend or identity provider.
Do not rely on client-only password strength hints or login throttling.

validation_steps: Test weak-password registration, repeated failed logins, and MFA-required flows to confirm policy enforcement is consistent.
Add regression tests for lockout or throttling behavior on sensitive auth endpoints.
Review admin, password-reset, and alternate login flows for weaker bypass paths.
unsafe_alternatives: Checking password strength only in the client.
Adding rate limiting to one login endpoint while leaving other auth paths unprotected.
Treating optional MFA banners as equivalent to enforced MFA policy.

references.label: OWASP Authentication Cheat Sheet
CWE-521 Weak Password Requirements

sources.label: OWASP Authentication Cheat Sheet
CWE-521 Weak Password Requirements

entries.title: Canonical remediation for missing CSRF protection
entries.canonical_fix_summary: Protect state-changing browser requests with anti-CSRF tokens or equivalent same-site defenses, and ensure sensitive actions cannot be triggered cross-origin by ambient browser credentials alone.

framework_variants.summary: Apply CSRF protection middleware or equivalent token checks on state-changing routes that rely on cookies or browser session state.
recommended_actions: Issue and validate per-session or per-request CSRF tokens on unsafe methods.
Set same-site cookie policy appropriately and keep token validation in one middleware path.

framework_variants.summary: Require anti-CSRF tokens or equivalent cookie-bound state protections on browser-authenticated mutating endpoints.
recommended_actions: Validate a CSRF token alongside session cookies on unsafe requests.
Use same-site and origin checks as defense-in-depth, not as the only control.

framework_variants.summary: Use Django's CSRF middleware correctly on all state-changing views and avoid exempting routes without a reviewed alternative.
recommended_actions: Keep CSRF middleware enabled on browser-authenticated forms and endpoints.
Review any exempted route and replace it with token or origin validation where possible.

framework_variants.summary: Keep Spring Security CSRF protections enabled for browser-authenticated flows and scope any exemptions narrowly.
recommended_actions: Protect state-changing endpoints with CSRF tokens when cookies are used for auth.
Review disabled or exempted routes for alternative request-integrity controls.

framework_variants.summary: Use antiforgery protections on cookie-authenticated mutating endpoints and keep exemptions rare and reviewed.
recommended_actions: Require antiforgery tokens for state-changing browser requests.
Combine same-site cookie settings with token validation rather than relying on either alone.

framework_variants.summary: Treat CSRF as a server-validated request-integrity control even if the client renders the forms or API calls.
recommended_actions: Send and validate antiforgery tokens on browser-authenticated mutating requests.
Do not treat hidden fields or frontend route guards as sufficient CSRF protection by themselves.

validation_steps: Attempt a cross-site form or request trigger and confirm the state-changing action is rejected without a valid token.
Add tests proving mutating routes fail when cookie-authenticated requests omit or forge the anti-CSRF token.
Review same-site, origin-check, and token behavior across all browser-authenticated state-changing flows.
unsafe_alternatives: Relying only on referer checks or client-side JavaScript restrictions.
Disabling CSRF middleware because the route is called by fetch or SPA code.
Treating hidden form fields without server validation as CSRF protection.

references.label: OWASP Cross-Site Request Forgery Prevention Cheat Sheet
CWE-352 Cross-Site Request Forgery

sources.label: OWASP Cross-Site Request Forgery Prevention Cheat Sheet
CWE-352 Cross-Site Request Forgery

entries.title: Canonical remediation for insecure CORS
entries.canonical_fix_summary: Restrict cross-origin access to explicit trusted origins, methods, and headers, and never combine wildcard or overly broad origin policy with credentialed responses.

framework_variants.summary: Configure CORS middleware with an explicit origin allow-list and keep credentialed requests limited to trusted callers.
recommended_actions: Replace wildcard origin policy with a reviewed allow-list.
Reject `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials` on broad or untrusted origin policy.

framework_variants.summary: Use CORS middleware with exact trusted origins and avoid permissive wildcard settings on sensitive APIs.
recommended_actions: Set explicit allowed origins, methods, and headers.
Do not enable credentials for arbitrary or wildcard origins.

framework_variants.summary: Constrain CORS configuration to trusted origins and keep credentialed browser access narrow and reviewed.
recommended_actions: Use an explicit allow-list for cross-origin callers.
Review environment-specific settings so development permissiveness does not leak into production.

framework_variants.summary: Apply narrow cross-origin policy in controller or global security config and keep credentialed origins explicitly enumerated.
recommended_actions: Define exact allowed origins and methods rather than permissive patterns.
Separate public-read cross-origin scenarios from authenticated credentialed APIs.

framework_variants.summary: Use named CORS policies with explicit trusted origins and avoid combining credentials with broad origin patterns.
recommended_actions: Replace permissive default CORS policy with reviewed named policies.
Apply the narrowest policy necessary for each endpoint group.

framework_variants.summary: Treat insecure CORS as a server/API policy problem; client code may reveal usage patterns but should not define the security boundary.
recommended_actions: Fix the API's CORS policy rather than trusting browser-side restrictions.
Review which origins genuinely need authenticated cross-origin access.

validation_steps: Test requests from untrusted origins and confirm sensitive responses no longer include permissive CORS headers.
Verify credentialed cross-origin requests succeed only for explicit trusted origins.
Review environment-specific configuration to ensure development wildcard settings are not active in production.
unsafe_alternatives: Keeping wildcard origins while allowing credentials.
Using broad regex or suffix matches without reviewing origin ownership risk.
Treating client-side fetch configuration as a substitute for API-side CORS policy.

references.label: OWASP Cross Origin Resource Sharing Cheat Sheet
CWE-942 Permissive Cross-domain Policy with Untrusted Domains

sources.label: OWASP Cross Origin Resource Sharing Cheat Sheet
CWE-942 Permissive Cross-domain Policy with Untrusted Domains

entries.title: Canonical remediation for unrestricted file upload
entries.canonical_fix_summary: Constrain uploads by type, size, content validation, and storage boundary, and ensure uploaded files cannot become executable, directly trusted, or broadly accessible without review.

framework_variants.summary: Validate upload type, size, and content server-side before storage, and keep uploads outside executable or public-by-default paths.
recommended_actions: Allow-list accepted MIME types and extensions, but also inspect content where practical.
Store uploads outside the web root and rename them to server-generated identifiers.

framework_variants.summary: Treat uploaded files as untrusted content and enforce type, size, scanning, and storage controls before any downstream use.
recommended_actions: Reject oversized or unexpected file types before writing them to durable storage.
Keep upload processing isolated from direct execution or templating paths.

framework_variants.summary: Constrain file uploads with validation, storage isolation, and safe serving policy so user content cannot become executable or directly trusted.
recommended_actions: Use reviewed validators and storage backends for allowed file classes only.
Serve uploads through controlled views or storage policies rather than raw executable paths.

framework_variants.summary: Validate multipart uploads strictly and isolate stored content from executable application paths and permissive content serving.
recommended_actions: Allow-list extensions and content types with size limits and content checks.
Keep uploaded files in non-executable storage locations with generated names.

framework_variants.summary: Apply strict file validation, storage isolation, and controlled serving for uploaded content before any file is accepted or exposed.
recommended_actions: Validate size, extension, and content expectations server-side.
Store files outside executable directories and use generated names or identifiers.

framework_variants.summary: Treat file upload protection as an API and storage concern; client-side accept filters are convenience only, not the security boundary.
recommended_actions: Validate uploads again in the server route or upstream API regardless of client hints.
Keep uploaded content out of executable or directly trusted server paths.

validation_steps: Attempt to upload disallowed file types, oversized files, and content mismatches and confirm the server rejects them.
Verify accepted uploads are stored outside executable or public-by-default paths with generated identifiers.
Review downstream processing flows for antivirus, image parsing, archive extraction, and direct file serving behavior.
unsafe_alternatives: Checking extensions only without validating size, content, or storage location.
Trusting client-side file filters as the main control.
Saving uploads in directly executable or publicly served application directories.

references.label: OWASP File Upload Cheat Sheet
CWE-434 Unrestricted Upload of File with Dangerous Type

sources.label: OWASP File Upload Cheat Sheet
CWE-434 Unrestricted Upload of File with Dangerous Type

entries.title: Canonical remediation for mass assignment
entries.canonical_fix_summary: Bind client input only to explicit allow-listed fields or DTOs, and prevent request payloads from directly controlling privileged, hidden, or integrity-sensitive model attributes.

framework_variants.summary: Map request bodies to explicit DTOs or allow-listed objects before persisting them to domain models.
recommended_actions: Pick only approved fields from the request body instead of spreading or assigning the full object.
Keep role, status, ownership, and other privileged attributes out of generic update binding.

framework_variants.summary: Use explicit request models for writable fields and keep internal or privileged attributes out of public input schemas.
recommended_actions: Separate public write DTOs from internal persistence models.
Reject extra or unexpected fields instead of silently binding them.

framework_variants.summary: Bind only approved fields in forms, serializers, and model update flows and avoid exposing privileged model attributes through generic request mapping.
recommended_actions: Set explicit writable fields in forms or serializers.
Keep ownership, permission, and status fields server-controlled.

framework_variants.summary: Bind request payloads into explicit DTOs rather than entities and control which fields can be updated through reviewed service logic.
recommended_actions: Use request DTOs with only approved writable fields.
Map privileged or integrity-sensitive attributes inside the service layer, not from the client payload.

framework_variants.summary: Use explicit view models or request contracts and avoid binding arbitrary request fields directly into domain entities.
recommended_actions: Restrict model binding to approved properties only.
Set sensitive attributes server-side after authorization and business checks.

framework_variants.summary: Treat mass-assignment protection as a server schema and binding concern, not a client form-shape guarantee.
recommended_actions: Validate and map writable fields again in the server action or API route.
Do not assume hidden or omitted form fields stay safe once a request is attacker-controlled.

validation_steps: Attempt to submit privileged or hidden fields in the request payload and confirm they are rejected or ignored safely.
Add regression tests proving only approved writable fields affect persisted state.
Review create, update, patch, and bulk-import flows for generic model binding patterns.
unsafe_alternatives: Hiding fields in the client while continuing to bind the full request object on the server.
Filtering a few dangerous fields but still allowing arbitrary extras.
Binding directly into persistence entities and hoping authorization later will correct it.

references.label: OWASP Mass Assignment Cheat Sheet
CWE-915 Improperly Controlled Modification of Dynamically-Determined Object Attributes

sources.label: OWASP Mass Assignment Cheat Sheet
CWE-915 Improperly Controlled Modification of Dynamically-Determined Object Attributes

entries.title: Canonical remediation for open redirect
entries.canonical_fix_summary: Replace attacker-controlled redirect targets with allow-listed route names or trusted destinations, and ensure user input cannot directly choose arbitrary external navigation targets.

framework_variants.summary: Resolve redirect destinations from server-side route identifiers or a strict allow-list instead of passing through user-provided URLs.
recommended_actions: Map request parameters to known-safe redirect targets rather than raw URLs.
Validate any remaining external redirect against an explicit allow-list before issuing it.

framework_variants.summary: Treat redirect targets as controlled application state and avoid building response redirects directly from request data.
recommended_actions: Translate user choices into trusted internal routes or approved destinations.
Reject arbitrary external redirect parameters unless they match a reviewed allow-list.

framework_variants.summary: Use named routes or safe redirect helpers and keep `next`-style parameters constrained to trusted local destinations.
recommended_actions: Validate redirect parameters against allowed hosts or internal paths only.
Prefer route names and server-side decision logic over user-supplied URLs.

framework_variants.summary: Resolve redirects from server-side route or state decisions and reject arbitrary destination URLs from request parameters.
recommended_actions: Use known-safe internal redirect targets where possible.
Allow-list any external destinations that genuinely need to be reachable.

framework_variants.summary: Use local redirect helpers and explicit host validation rather than redirecting to request-controlled destinations.
recommended_actions: Prefer local route or return-url helpers that enforce trusted destinations.
Reject or normalize any external redirect parameter that is not explicitly approved.

framework_variants.summary: Treat open redirects as a server routing and trust-boundary issue; client navigation hints do not replace server validation.
recommended_actions: Validate redirect targets in the server handler or middleware that performs the redirect.
Avoid trusting query-string navigation targets without an allow-list.

validation_steps: Attempt to supply an arbitrary external redirect target and confirm the application rejects it or routes to a safe default.
Add tests for local-only redirect enforcement, allowed-host logic, and malicious external URLs.
Review login, logout, payment, and post-action return flows for the same redirect pattern.
unsafe_alternatives: Checking only for a missing protocol while still allowing attacker-controlled hosts.
Trying to strip dangerous substrings from a redirect URL instead of validating the full destination.
Assuming client-side link construction prevents server-side redirect abuse.

references.label: OWASP Unvalidated Redirects and Forwards Cheat Sheet
CWE-601 URL Redirection to Untrusted Site

sources.label: OWASP Unvalidated Redirects and Forwards Cheat Sheet
CWE-601 URL Redirection to Untrusted Site

entries.title: Canonical remediation for XXE
entries.canonical_fix_summary: Disable external entity resolution and unsafe DTD processing in XML parsers that handle untrusted input, and keep XML handling constrained to reviewed parsers and safe configuration defaults.

framework_variants.summary: Avoid unsafe XML parsing libraries or parser defaults that allow external entities and DTD expansion on user-controlled input.
recommended_actions: Disable external entity resolution and DTD processing explicitly.
Move untrusted XML handling behind one reviewed parser configuration.

framework_variants.summary: Constrain XML parsing to safe libraries and parser settings that reject external entities and dangerous XML features.
recommended_actions: Disable external entity and DTD handling for all request-derived XML.
Prefer data-only formats when XML is not required by the integration.

framework_variants.summary: Treat untrusted XML as a high-risk format and keep parsing behind safe library configuration that disables external entities.
recommended_actions: Use hardened parser settings or safe XML libraries for all inbound XML paths.
Review file import, SSO, and webhook-style XML handlers for unsafe defaults.

framework_variants.summary: Configure XML parsers, transformers, and marshallers to disable external entities and DTD processing across the stack.
recommended_actions: Apply secure parser configuration centrally rather than per-controller drift.
Audit JAXB, SAX, DOM, and transformer flows for unsafe entity resolution.

framework_variants.summary: Use secure XML reader settings that prohibit DTD processing and external resource resolution for untrusted XML.
recommended_actions: Apply safe `XmlReaderSettings` or equivalent defaults across XML entry points.
Avoid parser fallbacks that silently re-enable dangerous XML features.

framework_variants.summary: Treat XXE as a server-side parser configuration issue in APIs and background processing, not a client-side validation problem.
recommended_actions: Fix XML parser configuration in the backend route or service that consumes the payload.
Do not trust client content restrictions as the XML security boundary.

validation_steps: Replay XML payloads with external entities and confirm the parser rejects them or treats them as inert text.
Add tests covering DTD-enabled, external-entity, and local-file resolution payloads.
Review every XML entry point, including file import and background jobs, for the same parser configuration.
unsafe_alternatives: Filtering a few entity strings while leaving parser entity resolution enabled.
Securing one XML endpoint while other parsers in the same service keep unsafe defaults.
Assuming trusted transport makes unsafe XML parser settings acceptable.

references.label: OWASP XML External Entity Prevention Cheat Sheet
CWE-611 Improper Restriction of XML External Entity Reference

sources.label: OWASP XML External Entity Prevention Cheat Sheet
CWE-611 Improper Restriction of XML External Entity Reference

entries.title: Canonical remediation for weak cryptography
entries.canonical_fix_summary: Replace broken or deprecated algorithms with modern approved primitives and secure modes, and centralize cryptographic choices so insecure defaults cannot linger in isolated code paths.

framework_variants.summary: Use modern Node crypto primitives and reviewed library defaults instead of deprecated hashes, ciphers, or weak modes.
recommended_actions: Replace MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, and similar legacy primitives with approved alternatives.
Prefer authenticated encryption modes and reviewed password-hashing functions where applicable.

framework_variants.summary: Use maintained cryptographic libraries with explicit modern algorithms and avoid custom or legacy crypto choices in request paths.
recommended_actions: Replace deprecated primitives with modern hash, encryption, or password storage algorithms.
Centralize crypto configuration so insecure library defaults are not scattered.

framework_variants.summary: Keep cryptographic operations on framework-safe defaults or reviewed libraries and avoid introducing legacy algorithms into application code.
recommended_actions: Replace unsafe hashes and ciphers in custom code or integrations.
Review token signing, password storage, and encryption helpers for outdated algorithm selection.

framework_variants.summary: Use approved modern algorithms through reviewed JCA/JCE or framework-safe configuration and avoid custom or deprecated primitives.
recommended_actions: Replace broken or legacy ciphers and hashes with approved current options.
Standardize cryptographic configuration across services to prevent downgrade paths.

framework_variants.summary: Use modern .NET cryptographic APIs and approved algorithms, and remove compatibility fallbacks that preserve weak primitives.
recommended_actions: Replace legacy hash and cipher usage with approved alternatives.
Keep encryption and signature configuration in one reviewed layer rather than ad hoc call sites.

framework_variants.summary: Treat weak cryptography as a server and platform concern in token handling, encryption services, and backend APIs rather than a client-only decision.
recommended_actions: Apply strong algorithms in the server code or identity system backing the client flow.
Do not rely on client-side crypto choices to compensate for weak server-side primitives.

validation_steps: Review the final code path and configuration to confirm deprecated algorithms and modes are no longer present.
Add tests or checks that assert the expected modern algorithms are selected in security-sensitive paths.
Review compatibility or migration code to ensure it does not silently re-enable weak cryptography.
unsafe_alternatives: Wrapping a weak primitive in more code instead of replacing it.
Keeping deprecated algorithms for convenience without hard migration boundaries.
Changing one call site while leaving the same weak algorithm active elsewhere in the stack.

references.label: OWASP Cryptographic Storage Cheat Sheet
CWE-327 Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm

sources.label: OWASP Cryptographic Storage Cheat Sheet
CWE-327 Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm

entries.title: Canonical remediation for insecure randomness
entries.canonical_fix_summary: Use a cryptographically secure source of randomness for security-sensitive values such as tokens, keys, reset links, and nonces, and prevent predictable PRNG output from influencing trust decisions.

framework_variants.summary: Use Node's cryptographically secure randomness APIs for tokens, identifiers, and secret material instead of predictable pseudo-random sources.
recommended_actions: Replace `Math.random()` and similar sources in security-sensitive paths.
Centralize token and nonce generation in one reviewed helper backed by secure randomness.

framework_variants.summary: Use secure randomness libraries or platform APIs for reset tokens, secrets, and other trust-bearing values.
recommended_actions: Replace predictable PRNG output in authentication or session flows.
Keep token generation behind one reviewed secure helper.

framework_variants.summary: Use framework-safe or secure platform randomness for security-sensitive values and avoid predictable custom generators.
recommended_actions: Replace non-cryptographic random sources in token, reset, and invite flows.
Review custom helpers for predictable output or reuse.

framework_variants.summary: Use secure random generation for authentication, session, and cryptographic support values rather than general-purpose PRNGs.
recommended_actions: Replace predictable token generation with secure random APIs.
Standardize secure randomness use in one reviewed utility layer.

framework_variants.summary: Use cryptographically secure random APIs for all trust-bearing tokens and secrets and remove predictable random generation from security flows.
recommended_actions: Replace general-purpose random classes in security-sensitive contexts.
Keep token and nonce generation centralized and reviewed.

framework_variants.summary: Treat secure randomness for trust-bearing values as a server concern; client-generated randomness should not define authentication or authorization truth.
recommended_actions: Generate sensitive tokens and secrets on the server using secure randomness.
Do not rely on client-side pseudo-random generation for security-critical values.

validation_steps: Review the final token or secret generation path and confirm it uses a cryptographically secure randomness source.
Add tests or assertions around helper selection so predictable PRNGs are not reintroduced.
Audit adjacent invite, reset, session, and nonce generation flows for the same weakness.
unsafe_alternatives: Keeping `Math.random()` or equivalent in security-sensitive code because the value looks long enough.
Hashing predictable randomness and treating it as secure.
Using secure randomness in one token flow while other trust-bearing values stay predictable.

references.label: OWASP Cryptographic Storage Cheat Sheet
CWE-330 Use of Insufficiently Random Values

sources.label: OWASP Cryptographic Storage Cheat Sheet
CWE-330 Use of Insufficiently Random Values

entries.title: Canonical remediation for NoSQL injection
entries.canonical_fix_summary: Constrain query structure to server-defined fields and operators, validate client input against strict schemas, and prevent attacker-controlled objects from directly shaping NoSQL query logic.

framework_variants.summary: Build NoSQL queries from explicit allow-listed fields and operators rather than passing through request-derived filter objects.
recommended_actions: Reject untrusted operators such as `$where` or unexpected nested filter structures.
Map request values into reviewed query builders instead of raw query objects.

framework_variants.summary: Bind input through explicit schemas and construct NoSQL filters from approved fields rather than deserialized request objects.
recommended_actions: Reject arbitrary operator injection and unexpected filter keys.
Keep database query construction inside one reviewed server-side helper layer.

framework_variants.summary: Constrain document or search-style query construction to approved fields and avoid passing user-controlled operators into backend query APIs.
recommended_actions: Validate filter payloads against strict schemas before query construction.
Prevent request bodies from directly shaping persistence-layer query objects.

framework_variants.summary: Construct NoSQL queries from reviewed DTO fields and block direct operator or query-object injection from request data.
recommended_actions: Allow-list supported filters and operators explicitly.
Keep raw query-object construction out of controllers and request mappers.

framework_variants.summary: Map request input into explicit query DTOs and build NoSQL filters from approved fields and operators only.
recommended_actions: Reject arbitrary operator keys and nested attacker-controlled query objects.
Centralize NoSQL query construction in one reviewed data-access layer.

framework_variants.summary: Treat NoSQL injection remediation as a server query-construction problem, not a client filter-shaping problem.
recommended_actions: Validate and rebuild filters in the server handler or API service.
Do not trust client-generated query objects as safe database input.

validation_steps: Replay malicious operator payloads such as `$where` or unexpected nested filters and confirm the server rejects or neutralizes them.
Add tests proving only approved filter fields and operators reach the NoSQL query builder.
Review search, list, admin, and reporting endpoints for the same raw filter-object pattern.
unsafe_alternatives: Filtering only one dangerous operator while still allowing arbitrary query-object structure.
Casting attacker-controlled objects to a different type without rebuilding the query safely.
Relying on the client to shape a safe filter object.

references.label: OWASP NoSQL Security Cheat Sheet
CWE-943 Improper Neutralization of Special Elements in Data Query Logic

sources.label: OWASP NoSQL Security Cheat Sheet
CWE-943 Improper Neutralization of Special Elements in Data Query Logic

entries.title: Canonical remediation for LDAP injection
entries.canonical_fix_summary: Escape or parameterize untrusted LDAP filter input and construct directory queries through safe APIs that keep attacker-controlled values separate from LDAP filter syntax.

framework_variants.summary: Escape request-derived directory values before they reach LDAP filter strings and keep filter structure server-controlled.
recommended_actions: Use safe LDAP escaping helpers on all untrusted filter values.
Build filters from reviewed templates rather than concatenated request data.

framework_variants.summary: Treat LDAP filter values as untrusted data and escape or bind them before any directory query is issued.
recommended_actions: Keep LDAP filter syntax server-defined and inject only escaped values.
Centralize directory-query construction in one reviewed helper.

framework_variants.summary: Escape untrusted LDAP input and keep directory search filters out of raw string concatenation paths.
recommended_actions: Use directory-safe escaping or parameterization for all user-derived values.
Review authentication and account-search flows for concatenated LDAP filters.

framework_variants.summary: Construct LDAP filters through reviewed templates or helpers and avoid injecting request data directly into filter syntax.
recommended_actions: Escape all untrusted values before filter construction.
Keep directory-query structure separate from attacker-controlled input.

framework_variants.summary: Use safe directory query construction and escape request-derived LDAP values before they reach the search filter.
recommended_actions: Build directory filters from server-controlled templates and escaped values.
Review authentication, lookup, and admin search flows for raw filter concatenation.

framework_variants.summary: Treat LDAP injection as a server-side directory query issue in auth and lookup services rather than a client validation issue.
recommended_actions: Escape and validate directory input in the backend service that issues LDAP queries.
Do not assume client restrictions keep directory search values safe.

validation_steps: Replay LDAP metacharacter payloads and confirm the filter remains structurally safe and semantically constrained.
Add tests proving user input is escaped or bound before reaching the LDAP query layer.
Review auth, directory search, and account-management flows for the same raw filter construction pattern.
unsafe_alternatives: Stripping a few special characters while leaving filter syntax attacker-influenced.
Escaping one field but continuing to concatenate other request-derived LDAP fragments.
Relying on UI restrictions to prevent malicious directory query input.

references.label: OWASP LDAP Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet
CWE-90 Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an LDAP Query

sources.label: OWASP LDAP Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet
CWE-90 Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an LDAP Query

entries.title: Remove hardcoded API keys and move them into managed secret storage
entries.canonical_fix_summary: Eliminate hardcoded API keys from source control, load them from managed secret storage or deployment-time configuration, and rotate any exposed credentials immediately.

framework_variants.summary: Load third-party API keys from environment or secret-management integrations during startup and keep them out of route handlers and checked-in config files.
recommended_actions: Read keys from deployment-managed secrets and fail startup if required credentials are missing.
Rotate any key that was previously committed and update calling services to use the new secret source.

framework_variants.summary: Inject API keys through settings or secret providers rather than embedding them in routers, dependencies, or module constants.
recommended_actions: Use configuration classes or secret stores to load API keys at runtime.
Remove committed keys from code, sample files, and notebooks, then rotate them.

framework_variants.summary: Keep API keys in environment-backed settings or a managed secret backend instead of hardcoding them in settings modules, views, or utility files.
recommended_actions: Load keys through settings that are populated from deployment secrets.
Review admin commands, integrations, and Celery tasks for copied or fallback credentials.

framework_variants.summary: Bind API credentials from secure configuration sources and keep them out of Java constants, YAML defaults, and example profiles committed to the repo.
recommended_actions: Use environment-backed properties or a secret manager for runtime injection.
Rotate exposed credentials and update any profile or bootstrap config that embedded the old values.

framework_variants.summary: Use configuration providers such as environment variables, user secrets, or managed secret stores rather than hardcoded API keys in services and appsettings files.
recommended_actions: Bind secrets from secure configuration at startup and avoid checked-in credential defaults.
Rotate previously exposed keys and audit deployment slots or test configs for duplicates.

framework_variants.summary: Never embed sensitive API keys in browser-delivered bundles; keep privileged keys on the server and proxy requests through controlled backend routes when needed.
recommended_actions: Move any privileged key usage to server-only code or backend services.
Rotate leaked keys and ensure public client configuration contains only non-sensitive publishable tokens.

validation_steps: Search the repository and build artifacts to confirm the old key is no longer present in source, configs, or bundles.
Verify the application still loads the credential correctly from managed configuration in each target environment.
Confirm the exposed key has been rotated or revoked and that downstream integrations use the replacement.
unsafe_alternatives: Base64-encoding or lightly obfuscating the key while still committing it to the repository.
Moving the key into a checked-in config file or client bundle and treating that as remediation.
Keeping the compromised key active after removing it from source.

references.label: OWASP Secrets Management Cheat Sheet
CWE-798 Use of Hard-coded Credentials

sources.label: OWASP Secrets Management Cheat Sheet
CWE-798 Use of Hard-coded Credentials

entries.title: Remove hardcoded passwords and replace them with managed authentication secrets
entries.canonical_fix_summary: Remove hardcoded passwords from code and configuration, replace them with managed secrets or delegated authentication flows, and rotate any password that may have been exposed.

framework_variants.summary: Keep database and service passwords in secret-managed runtime configuration instead of source files, route handlers, or scripts.
recommended_actions: Load credentials from environment or secret providers and fail closed when they are missing.
Rotate any password that was committed and update integration tests to use safe local fixtures.

framework_variants.summary: Inject passwords through settings or secret backends rather than embedding them in modules, dependency wiring, or startup code.
recommended_actions: Move credentials into deployment-managed secrets and reference them via runtime configuration.
Rotate exposed passwords and review notebooks, test files, and sample configs for copies.

framework_variants.summary: Avoid hardcoded service or admin passwords in settings, commands, fixtures, and management utilities; source them from secret-backed settings instead.
recommended_actions: Use environment-backed settings or a secret manager for credential loading.
Review superuser bootstrap scripts and integration helpers for embedded passwords.

framework_variants.summary: Remove embedded passwords from properties, Java constants, and bootstrap code, and bind them from secure configuration at runtime.
recommended_actions: Use secret-backed property sources instead of committing credentials in configuration files.
Rotate previously exposed passwords and update deployment manifests to consume the new secret source.

framework_variants.summary: Keep passwords in secure configuration providers or external secret stores rather than source files, connection strings, or test defaults.
recommended_actions: Bind credentials from environment or managed secrets during startup.
Rotate committed passwords and verify local development uses safe non-production secrets.

framework_variants.summary: Do not place passwords or privileged login secrets in frontend code; move authenticated operations to server-only routes or backend services.
recommended_actions: Remove any password-like values from browser code and environment files that are exposed to the client.
Rotate leaked credentials and redesign flows that depended on client-held shared secrets.

validation_steps: Search source, history-relevant config, and deployment manifests to confirm the old password is no longer present.
Verify the application authenticates correctly using the managed secret source in each environment.
Confirm the exposed password has been rotated or invalidated and cannot be used anymore.
unsafe_alternatives: Renaming the variable or moving the password into another committed file.
Replacing the password with a reversible encoding while still shipping it with the application.
Keeping the compromised password active after removing it from the main code path.

references.label: OWASP Secrets Management Cheat Sheet
CWE-259 Use of Hard-coded Password

sources.label: OWASP Secrets Management Cheat Sheet
CWE-259 Use of Hard-coded Password

entries.title: Remove hardcoded tokens and reissue them through controlled secret delivery
entries.canonical_fix_summary: Replace hardcoded bearer tokens, session tokens, and integration tokens with managed secret delivery, and revoke or rotate any token that was exposed in source or build artifacts.

framework_variants.summary: Keep service tokens in secret-backed configuration and inject them into integrations at runtime instead of embedding them in route code or utility modules.
recommended_actions: Load tokens from managed secrets and centralize access in a small configuration layer.
Revoke committed tokens and update integration clients to read the replacement from secure config.

framework_variants.summary: Source tokens from runtime settings or secret providers rather than module-level constants, dependencies, or test helpers that ship with the app.
recommended_actions: Move token values into secret-backed settings and avoid checked-in defaults.
Rotate leaked tokens and review async workers or webhook clients for duplicate storage.

framework_variants.summary: Remove embedded tokens from settings, integrations, management commands, and background jobs, and retrieve them from secret-managed configuration.
recommended_actions: Use environment-backed settings or a secret backend for token retrieval.
Revoke exposed tokens and update scheduled tasks and third-party integrations to use the new source.

framework_variants.summary: Keep integration tokens out of committed properties and Java code, and bind them from secure property sources or secret managers at runtime.
recommended_actions: Use environment-backed configuration or secret stores to inject tokens.
Rotate any leaked token and remove copies from profiles, examples, and bootstrap code.

framework_variants.summary: Use configuration providers or managed secret stores for token delivery instead of hardcoded constants, appsettings values, or client classes.
recommended_actions: Read tokens from secure configuration during startup or service creation.
Revoke exposed tokens and scan deployment settings and test data for residual copies.

framework_variants.summary: Do not place privileged tokens in frontend code or public environment variables; keep token-bearing integrations on the server or backend edge.
recommended_actions: Move privileged API calls to server-only handlers and protect tokens in server-side secret storage.
Revoke leaked tokens and verify no secret token is exposed in client bundles or browser storage.

validation_steps: Search the repository, generated bundles, and environment templates to confirm the old token is gone.
Verify integrations still function when the token is loaded from managed configuration.
Confirm the exposed token has been revoked or rotated and cannot be used successfully.
unsafe_alternatives: Keeping the token in a checked-in environment file or sample configuration.
Masking part of the token while continuing to ship the usable secret with the application.
Removing the token from source but leaving the compromised token active.

references.label: OWASP Secrets Management Cheat Sheet
CWE-798 Use of Hard-coded Credentials

sources.label: OWASP Secrets Management Cheat Sheet
CWE-798 Use of Hard-coded Credentials

entries.title: Replace hardcoded secrets with managed secret distribution and rotation
entries.canonical_fix_summary: Move generic hardcoded secrets such as signing keys, shared secrets, and service credentials into managed secret storage, remove them from code and artifacts, and rotate any exposed values.

framework_variants.summary: Keep app secrets such as signing keys and integration credentials in environment or secret stores rather than source files and middleware definitions.
recommended_actions: Load secrets during startup from a managed provider and keep access centralized.
Rotate committed secrets and review tests, scripts, and example configs for reused values.

framework_variants.summary: Read signing keys and shared secrets from runtime settings or a secret manager instead of constants in modules, dependencies, or startup code.
recommended_actions: Use secret-backed settings for runtime injection and fail startup when critical secrets are missing.
Rotate any exposed secret and audit workers, task queues, and examples for copies.

framework_variants.summary: Keep `SECRET_KEY`-style values and other shared secrets out of committed settings and move them to secret-backed configuration sources.
recommended_actions: Load framework and integration secrets from environment-backed settings or secret managers.
Rotate exposed secrets and invalidate any sessions, signatures, or tokens derived from them if required.

framework_variants.summary: Bind signing keys and shared secrets from secure property sources rather than embedding them in code, property defaults, or sample profiles.
recommended_actions: Use external secret stores or environment-backed properties for secret injection.
Rotate leaked secrets and review any signed-token or crypto flows that depended on the compromised value.

framework_variants.summary: Source application secrets from secure configuration providers instead of hardcoded constants, checked-in settings, or test bootstrap files.
recommended_actions: Use managed secret delivery for signing keys and shared credentials.
Rotate compromised secrets and review derived tokens, cookies, or signatures for invalidation needs.

framework_variants.summary: Never expose sensitive shared secrets to browser code; keep secret-bearing logic on the server and inject values only into server-side runtime configuration.
recommended_actions: Move secret-dependent operations to server-only code paths.
Rotate any exposed secret and confirm no usable value remains in client bundles, source maps, or public env files.

validation_steps: Search source, generated assets, and deployment templates to confirm the secret value no longer appears.
Verify the application starts and operates correctly using the managed secret source.
Confirm the compromised secret has been rotated and any dependent trust artifacts have been reviewed or invalidated.
unsafe_alternatives: Moving the secret into another committed file or public environment variable.
Using reversible obfuscation instead of real secret management.
Leaving the leaked secret valid after removing it from the codebase.

references.label: OWASP Secrets Management Cheat Sheet
CWE-798 Use of Hard-coded Credentials

sources.label: OWASP Secrets Management Cheat Sheet
CWE-798 Use of Hard-coded Credentials

entries.title: Enforce object-level authorization on every resource access path
entries.canonical_fix_summary: Apply server-side object-level authorization checks on every read, update, delete, and action path so users can access only resources they are explicitly allowed to use.

framework_variants.summary: Authorize each object lookup and mutation in route handlers and service layers instead of trusting route parameters, hidden fields, or client filtering.
recommended_actions: Bind resource access to the authenticated principal before returning or mutating data.
Review direct ID-based endpoints and nested resource actions for missing ownership or entitlement checks.

framework_variants.summary: Perform object-level authorization in dependencies or service logic before using path, query, or body identifiers to fetch or change records.
recommended_actions: Check that the current principal is allowed to access the target object before any business action proceeds.
Review read, update, delete, and export flows that accept user-controlled identifiers.

framework_variants.summary: Enforce per-object authorization in views, DRF permissions, and querysets rather than assuming model exposure or URL structure is safe.
recommended_actions: Filter querysets by the current user or tenant and verify ownership before returning objects.
Review detail views, form posts, and admin-like actions for unscoped primary-key access.

framework_variants.summary: Apply object-level access control in controllers and services before performing repository lookups or state changes driven by request identifiers.
recommended_actions: Tie resource access to the authenticated subject or granted authorities before use.
Review endpoint methods that accept IDs or references and reach straight into repositories.

framework_variants.summary: Check per-resource authorization in controllers, handlers, and services before acting on route or payload identifiers.
recommended_actions: Enforce ownership, tenant, or policy checks before returning or modifying the target resource.
Review API endpoints and Razor or MVC actions that trust user-supplied IDs.

framework_variants.summary: Treat IDOR as a backend authorization problem and ensure API routes or backing services enforce object access regardless of what IDs the client can send.
recommended_actions: Keep authorization checks on the server for all object fetch and mutation paths.
Do not rely on hidden IDs, disabled controls, or client route guarding as the primary protection.

validation_steps: Attempt to access peer or cross-tenant object identifiers and confirm the server denies unauthorized access.
Add automated tests covering read, update, delete, and action endpoints with both authorized and unauthorized principals.
Review logs and business workflows to ensure object-level checks happen before side effects.
unsafe_alternatives: Relying on unguessable IDs without server-side authorization checks.
Filtering object lists in the UI while leaving direct object endpoints unprotected.
Checking authorization after a state-changing action has already occurred.

references.label: OWASP Authorization Cheat Sheet
CWE-639 Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key

sources.label: OWASP Authorization Cheat Sheet
CWE-639 Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key

entries.title: Stop logging sensitive data and sanitize observability output
entries.canonical_fix_summary: Remove secrets, tokens, credentials, and other sensitive values from logs, traces, and error telemetry, and apply structured redaction so observability output remains useful without leaking protected data.

framework_variants.summary: Redact sensitive request fields, headers, and downstream responses before they reach log middleware, error handlers, or trace exporters.
recommended_actions: Add structured redaction for secrets, auth headers, tokens, and personal data in logger configuration.
Review error handlers, debug logs, and third-party instrumentation for unsafe payload logging.

framework_variants.summary: Keep request bodies, auth material, and exception details out of application logs unless they are explicitly sanitized and minimally scoped.
recommended_actions: Configure logging and exception handling to redact or omit sensitive fields.
Review middleware, dependency debugging, and background-task logs for raw payload output.

framework_variants.summary: Redact secrets and personal data from request logging, admin actions, and exception telemetry instead of dumping raw request or model content.
recommended_actions: Use logging filters to strip credentials, tokens, and sensitive form fields.
Review custom middleware, signals, and audit logs for unsanitized user or credential data.

framework_variants.summary: Use structured logging with redaction rules so headers, tokens, request bodies, and exception payloads do not leak sensitive values.
recommended_actions: Configure logging frameworks and interceptors to mask secrets and personal data.
Review debug-level logging, filters, and actuator-style endpoints for accidental disclosure.

framework_variants.summary: Apply logging filters and structured redaction to prevent credentials, tokens, and personal data from reaching logs, traces, or error telemetry.
recommended_actions: Mask sensitive fields in middleware and logger enrichers before emission.
Review exception handlers, model dumps, and request logging for unsafe serialization.

framework_variants.summary: Avoid logging secrets, tokens, or sensitive user data in client telemetry or server-rendered route logs, and keep browser diagnostics free of privileged values.
recommended_actions: Redact sensitive values before sending client telemetry or server-side route logs.
Review console logs, error reporting, and analytics payloads for credential or personal-data leakage.

validation_steps: Exercise sensitive flows and confirm logs, traces, and error telemetry do not contain secrets or protected data.
Add tests or log snapshots proving redaction occurs for known sensitive fields and headers.
Review retention and downstream log sinks to ensure historical leakage is addressed where feasible.
unsafe_alternatives: Relying on log access controls alone while still emitting raw sensitive values.
Masking only one field while related tokens, headers, or payload fragments remain exposed.
Using debug logging in production for sensitive flows without redaction.

references.label: OWASP Logging Cheat Sheet
CWE-532 Insertion of Sensitive Information into Log File

sources.label: OWASP Logging Cheat Sheet
CWE-532 Insertion of Sensitive Information into Log File

entries.title: Add trustworthy audit events for security-relevant actions
entries.canonical_fix_summary: Emit structured, tamper-aware audit events for security-relevant actions such as authentication changes, privilege changes, administrative actions, and sensitive data access, and route them to a monitored audit trail.

framework_variants.summary: Add structured audit events around auth, admin, and data-sensitive actions in middleware or service boundaries rather than relying on ad hoc console logging.
recommended_actions: Capture actor, action, target, outcome, and timestamp in an audit-specific sink.
Review admin routes, role changes, credential events, and export flows for missing audit coverage.

framework_variants.summary: Record audit events in service logic or dependencies for privileged actions, account changes, and sensitive record access using a dedicated audit schema.
recommended_actions: Emit structured audit records with consistent fields and outcome metadata.
Review async tasks, auth flows, and admin endpoints for important actions that currently leave no trail.

framework_variants.summary: Use explicit audit logging for admin actions, permission changes, auth events, and high-risk data access rather than depending only on generic application logs.
recommended_actions: Create structured audit records with actor, target, action, and result details.
Review admin, DRF, signals, and management commands for missing audit coverage.

framework_variants.summary: Emit structured audit events from controllers or services for security-relevant actions and route them to an auditable sink separate from noisy application logs.
recommended_actions: Capture who did what, to which resource, when, and with what result.
Review role changes, authentication flows, administrative APIs, and sensitive exports for missing events.

framework_variants.summary: Add structured audit events in handlers, controllers, and services for privileged operations and sensitive access paths, and store them in a monitored audit trail.
recommended_actions: Emit actor, action, target, and outcome details in an audit-specific sink.
Review auth, admin, policy, and data-export flows for important actions that currently are not recorded.

framework_variants.summary: Treat audit gaps as a server-side responsibility: API routes and backend services should record privileged actions, not the browser alone.
recommended_actions: Emit audit events from server actions, API routes, or backing services for sensitive operations.
Do not rely on client analytics or console logs as the authoritative audit trail.

validation_steps: Trigger representative security-relevant actions and confirm structured audit events are emitted with the expected fields.
Verify audit records are stored or forwarded to the intended monitored sink and are distinguishable from ordinary app logs.
Review failure paths and denied actions to ensure important negative outcomes are also captured where policy requires.
unsafe_alternatives: Relying only on generic debug or access logs without structured audit semantics.
Recording audit events only on success while omitting denied or failed high-risk actions.
Keeping audit data solely in client telemetry or ephemeral console output.

references.label: OWASP Logging Cheat Sheet
CWE-778 Insufficient Logging

sources.label: OWASP Logging Cheat Sheet
CWE-778 Insufficient Logging

entries.title: Neutralize stored XSS with contextual output encoding and safe rendering
